proof: [§31] The slave was doubtless promised his freedom: it was certainly to the prosecution alone that he could look for release from his sufferings. Probably both of these considerations induced him to make the false charges against me which he did; he hoped to gain his freedom, and his one immediate wish was to end the torture.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text discusses the standard Athenian legal procedure where a slave's testimony was only considered valid if extracted through judicial torture (basanos).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This was a standard legal rule affecting the entire enslaved population in judicial contexts.

proof: [§96] Acquit me, then, today and at the trial for murder, the prosecution shall take the traditional oath before accusing me: you shall decide my case by the laws of the land.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references the 'diomosia', a traditional oath required by law in murder trials to ensure the accuser's accountability.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those involved in homicide litigation, a subset of the population.

proof: [§3] See how the Spartans bear whipping, after they have learned that it is a reasonable thing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author cites the Spartan custom of ritualized or educational flogging as a recognized societal norm where bodily integrity is sacrificed for training.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a cultural norm applicable to a specific city-state's citizens.

proof: [§3] Shall kinship to Caesar, or any other of the great at Rome, enable a man to live secure, above contempt, and void of all fear whatever
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies a concrete societal reality where legal and social security is tied to imperial lineage or proximity to power.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to an extremely small elite circle within the Roman Empire.

proof: [§3] Priscus Helvidius, too, saw, and acted accordingly; for when Vespasian had sent to forbid his going to the Senate, he answered, It is in your power to prevent my continuing a senator; but while I am one I must go.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage illustrates that holding the status of senator carries an inherent right and duty to participate in the assembly, which the senator defends even against imperial prohibition.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Relates to the senatorial class, a specific high-status group.

proof: [§3] To banishment, says he, or to death? To banishment. What of my estate? It is not taken away. Well, then, let us go as far as Aricia, and dine there.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The dialogue regarding Agrippinus shows that in certain instances of Roman banishment, the law permitted the exiled individual to retain his property/estate.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to individuals specifically sentenced to 'relegatio' or milder forms of banishment.

proof: [§3] But I have them by right of purchase, and not they me. Do you see what it is you regard? Your regards look downward towards the earth, and what is lower than earth, and towards the unjust laws of men long dead
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus critiques current Roman laws that grant owners absolute rights over individuals through purchase, treating people as property.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Slave ownership was a widespread legal status in the Roman world.

proof: [§3] To this God you likewise ought to swear such an oath as the soldiers do to Caesar. For they, in order to receive their pay, swear to prefer before all things the safety of Caesar
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes the legal requirement for soldiers to swear an oath to the Emperor as a prerequisite for receiving their wages.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the entire professional military apparatus.

proof: [§4] Why are not you willing to stretch out your neck, like Lateranus, when he was commanded by Nero to be beheaded? For, shrinking a little after receiving a weak blow, he stretched it out again. [...] I will throw you into prison; I will behead that paltry body of yours.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the historical reality of the Roman Emperor (Nero) exercising absolute power over the lives of citizens, including high-ranking officials like Lateranus, through summary execution.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to a single individual holding the highest office (the Emperor/Caesar).

proof: [§5] This Priscus Helvidius, too, saw, and acted accordingly; for when Vespasian had sent to forbid his going to the Senate, he answered, It is in your power to prevent my continuing a senator; but while I am one I must go.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The dialogue establishes that holding the status of a Senator carries a concrete institutional right and obligation to attend the Senate, which the Senator asserts even against the Emperor's wishes.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This pertains to the senatorial class, a small, elite segment of Roman society.

proof: [§5] Come now, Epictetus, take off your beard. If I am a philosopher, I answer, I will not take it off.—Then I will take off your head.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage implies that the beard was a fundamental marker of the philosopher's identity in Roman society, and that preserving it was a matter of professional integrity even under threat of death.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the specific sub-group of individuals identified as philosophers.

proof: [§4] To banishment, says he, or to death? To banishment. What of my estate? It is not taken away.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The inquiry into whether an estate was 'taken away' upon condemnation shows that the Roman state/Emperor had the legal power to confiscate property as part of criminal or political sentencing.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Refers to the unique power of the sovereign/state authority.

proof: [§5] Thus, it is reasonable for one man to submit to a menial office, who considers this only, that if he does not submit to it he shall be whipt and lose his dinner.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text uses the threat of being 'whipt' and losing 'dinner' as a standard consequence for a person in a 'menial office' (slave or low-status servant) who fails to perform their duties.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the large population of slaves and low-status laborers in the Roman Empire.

proof: [§4] when Epaphroditus, the freedman of Nero, interrogated him about the conspiracy, If I have a mind to say anything, replied he, I will tell it to your master.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text notes that Epaphroditus, despite being a freedman, had the authority to interrogate a high-status individual (Lateranus) on behalf of the Emperor, illustrating the unique power delegated to imperial freedmen.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While applying to freedmen generally, the specific power mentioned was concentrated in the small circle of imperial freedmen.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to self-defense or dignified speech in court/prison
proof: [§7] The only real thing is to study how to rid life of lamentation, and complaint, and Alas! and I am undone, and misfortune, and failure; and to learn what death, what exile, what a prison, what poison is; that he may be able to say in a prison, like Socrates, My dear Crito, if it thus pleases the gods, thus let it be; and not, Wretched old man, have I kept my gray hairs for this!
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus references the historical/legal reality of Socrates to establish a standard for how a person in the legal status of a prisoner is permitted to conduct themselves. While framed philosophically, it relies on the known civic precedent of Socrates being allowed to speak and receive visitors in prison.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to individuals under specific legal duress (imprisonment), a subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to profit from intellectual property
proof: [§7] Do not you know that the whole volume is sold for five denarii? Does he who comments upon it, then, value himself at more than that sum?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates a market reality in the Roman Empire where philosophical treatises (like those of Chrysippus) have a fixed market value (five denarii). Epictetus argues that the person merely commenting on the text does not gain value beyond the physical book's price, reflecting a lack of high-status economic protection for derivative intellectual labor.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to a specific professional class of scholars/commentators in urban centers.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom to speak and act in public
proof: [§8] Shall I call this strength of mind? By no means,—unless we allow it to be such in the vilest debauchees publicly to speak and act out their worse impulses.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus implies that society does not (and should not) allow individuals categorized as 'debauchees' to act out their impulses in public spaces. This reflects a customary or legal restriction on public behavior for those seen as morally/socially deviant.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to a specific category of social outcasts/deviants.

proof: [§7] Take the treatise on the active powers, and see how thoroughly I have perused it. I do not inquire into this, O slavish man, but how you exert those powers...
extracted_rules.reasoning: By addressing a student as 'slavish man' (or literally a slave/person of slave character) who has perused a treatise, the text confirms that even those in or associated with a 'slavish' state had the resource of access to complex philosophical texts/literacy in this society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to the broad category of the enslaved or those of low social standing who were often educated in the Roman period.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be perceived as virtuous and honorable
proof: [§9] I undertake to show you that you have means and faculties to exhibit greatness of soul, and a manly spirit; but what occasion you have to find fault and complain, do you show me if you can.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus frames 'manly spirit' and 'greatness of soul' as faculties that men must exercise to avoid 'baseness' and 'impiety'. Failing to use these faculties for endurance results in a loss of social and moral standing, implying a strict cultural expectation that men must not complain about hardship.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the general class of men and citizens in Stoic society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to engage in dialectic and conversation
proof: [§10] And yet who beside the wise man is capable of treating an argument, or who beside is sagacious in reasoning, and incapable of being deceived and imposed on by sophistry?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies the 'wise man' (the philosopher) as the only group with the capacity and the implicit right to properly engage in high-level reasoning and handle sophistry, distinguishing them from the 'unthinking' who are easily embarrassed.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a very specific, small subgroup of society: those trained in philosophy and dialectic.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from external restraint of the will
proof: [§9] And yet God has not only granted these faculties by which we may bear every event without being depressed or broken by it, but, like a good prince and a true father, has placed their exercise above restraint, compulsion, or hindrance, and wholly within our own control; nor has he reserved a power, even to himself, of hindering or restraining them.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus describes a fundamental rule of the human condition in his society: while the body might be constrained, the faculty of the will and the pursuit of virtue are legally and naturally 'above restraint' or 'compulsion'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all human beings as defined by the author's philosophical framework.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from reprimand for minor errors
proof: [§10] But suppose, after all, I should make a mistake in these points,—it is not as if I had killed my father. O slavish man, in this case you had no father to kill. but the only fault that you could commit in this instance, you have committed. This very thing I myself said to Rufus when he reproved me for not finding the weak point in some syllogism.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that within the scholarly/philosophical community, intellectual errors (failing to find a weak point in a syllogism) are treated as serious 'faults' (reproofs), even if they do not reach the level of capital crimes like parricide.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This concerns the specific interactions between masters and students in philosophical schools.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to life-sustaining resources through self-effort
proof: [§12] But wherewithal shall I be fed? For I have nothing. To what do fugitive slaves trust when they run away from their masters? Is it to their estates,—their servants,—their plate? To nothing but themselves. Yet they do not fail to obtain the necessaries of life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus notes that even fugitive slaves, who have been stripped of legal support and property, are able to sustain their lives through their own agency, suggesting a social reality where basic survival was possible for this group outside of formal structures.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a large class of people (slaves/fugitives) and their basic survival.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from judicial and tyrannical coercion
proof: [§12] Here thieves and robbers, courts and tyrants, claim power over us, through the body and its possessions. Suffer us to show them that they have no power.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text acknowledges that as a matter of civic reality, courts and 'tyrants' (political authorities) exercise concrete power over the physical bodies and properties of individuals in the society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule describes the general vulnerability of all subjects/citizens to the legal and political apparatus.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold administrative office upon return
proof: [§13] I know a man older than I am, who is now a commissary at Rome. When he passed through this place, on his return from exile, what an account did he give me of his former life [...] Yet, after all, how did he act? Before he entered the city he was met by a letter from Caesar. On receiving it he forgot all his former resolutions; and has ever since been accumulating affairs upon himself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of a man returning from exile and immediately being appointed to a commissary position by Caesar shows that the status of 'exile' could be fully reversed, restoring access to high-level political and economic administrative roles.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to high-status individuals who have been exiled and then pardoned or recalled to court.

proof: [§13] Is there any likeness, then, between reading a petition, I entreat you to give me a permission to export corn, and this, I entreat you to learn from Chrysippus what the administration of the universe is
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references the practice of petitioning for 'permission to export corn,' indicating a concrete trade restriction where individuals do not have the inherent right to export agricultural goods without state approval.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to merchants and landowners involved in the grain trade.

extracted_rules.resource: Right of admittance to the imperial court
proof: [§13] When you are once within reach of Rome you will forget all this; and if you can but once gain admittance to court, you will be rejoiced and thank God.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Admittance to the court is treated as a specific resource that grants political influence and status to the 'politicians' or 'commissaries' seeking preferment at Rome.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is limited to a very small circle of the political elite in Rome.

proof: [§96] Acquit me, then, today and at the trial for murder, the prosecution shall take the traditional oath before accusing me: you shall decide my case by the laws of the land.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references a specific procedural requirement where a prosecutor must take a 'traditional oath' (diomosia) before the defendant can be legally charged in a murder trial, serving as a safeguard for the accused.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule is highly specific as it applies only to a particular sub-set of legal cases (murder trials) and a specific role (defendant).

proof: [§31] The slave was doubtless promised his freedom: it was certainly to the prosecution alone that he could look for release from his sufferings. Probably both of these considerations induced him to make the false charges against me which he did; he hoped to gain his freedom, and his one immediate wish was to end the torture.
extracted_rules.reasoning: In the context of the author's legal system, a slave's testimony was only valid or extracted through judicial torture ('basanos'), meaning they lacked the right to provide evidence as free men did.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire class of enslaved people in this society when involved in legal proceedings.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of emotional expression in public
proof: [§14] I mean some such motive as induced a person at Rome to hide his face while a horse was running to which he earnestly wished success; and when, beyond his expectation, it won the race he was obliged himself to be sponged, to recover from his faintness.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus describes a specific social norm in Rome where a person of status (implied by his presence at the races and his 'faintness' requiring attendants) felt compelled to hide his face to mask his emotional investment in a public spectacle, indicating a restriction on open behavior for high-status individuals.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule concerns a specific social setting (the races) and a specific class of people (those with a public reputation to maintain).

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom from responsibility for offspring's actions
proof: [§15] Why do not you rather thank the gods that they have made you superior to those events which they have not placed within your own control, and have rendered you accountable for that only which is within your own control? They discharge you from all responsibility for your parents, for your brothers, for your body, possessions, death, life.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus outlines a principle (likely reflecting Stoic-influenced legal or moral norms of the time) where individuals are not held accountable or 'responsible' for the actions or states of their family members, defining the boundaries of legal and moral personhood.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This is a general rule defining the nature of individual responsibility for almost all adults in the society.

proof: [§16] And if you call for hot water, and your servant does not hear you, or, if he does, brings it only warm, or perhaps is not to be found at home, then to abstain from anger or petulance, is not this to the divine acceptance? ... But I have them by right of purchase, and not they me.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus acknowledges the legal reality of the 'right of purchase' over slaves and their obligation to perform domestic tasks like fetching water. While he argues for moral patience from the master, he reinforces the legal status of the slave as a purchased resource subject to a master's domestic commands.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire enslaved population in Roman society, which was a very large and clearly defined demographic.

proof: [§16] But I have them by right of purchase, and not they me. Do you see what it is you regard? Your regards look downward towards the earth, and what is lower than earth, and towards the unjust laws of men long dead.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions the 'right of purchase' and refers to the 'laws of men' that establish ownership of people. Even though the author critiques these laws from a Stoic perspective, he confirms they are the functioning legal rules of his society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Slave ownership was widespread among the wealthy and middle classes of the Roman Empire.

proof: [§17] To this God you likewise ought to swear such an oath as the soldiers do to Caesar. For they, in order to receive their pay, swear to prefer before all things the safety of Caesar.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a concrete legal/military requirement: soldiers must swear a specific oath of loyalty to the Emperor as a prerequisite to receiving their wages (stipendium).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule is specific to the military profession, which was a distinct and significant subset of the population.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be addressed as a male
proof: [§19] Does not nature in each of us call out, even at a distance, I am a man; approach and address me as such; inquire no further; see the characteristic? ... Therefore we ought to preserve the characteristics made by the Creator; we ought not to reject them, nor confound, as much as in us lies, the distinct sexes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus frames the wearing of a beard not just as a natural fact but as a social rule to maintain the 'distinct sexes.' He argues that being recognized and addressed correctly as a man is a right/duty tied to these physical markers.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to one half of the entire human population.

proof: [§19] But as soldiers are furnished ready for their commander, shod, clothed, and armed,—for it would be a grievous thing for a colonel to be obliged to go through his regiment to put on their clothes.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author uses the Roman military's logistical system—where soldiers are provided with shoes, clothes, and arms—as an analogy for nature. This reflects the actual practice of the Roman state providing gear to its troops.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies specifically to the Roman legions and auxiliary forces.

proof: [§21] Why, after all, should we be angry at the multitude? They are thieves and robbers. [...] What, then; ought not this thief and this adulterer to be destroyed?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references a standard societal expectation that individuals categorized as thieves or adulterers are subject to being 'destroyed' (executed or killed), which Epictetus argues against from a philosophical standpoint, thereby confirming the existing social/legal norm of the time.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to specific sub-groups of the population defined by their illegal activities/occupations as perceived by the law.

proof: [§21] But the tyrant will chain—what? A leg. He will take away—what? A head.
[§22] Thus, when the tyrant says to any one, I will chain your leg, he who chiefly values his leg cries out for pity.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author describes the real-world power of political tyrants in his era to arbitrarily imprison (chain) or execute (take a head) those under their rule.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Under a tyranny, this rule applies to almost any member of the society regardless of other status.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to be treated as a human
proof: [§22] Epaphroditus owned a shoemaker, whom, because he was good for nothing, he sold. This very fellow being, by some strange luck, bought by a courtier, became shoemaker to Caesar.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the common practice of slaves being owned and sold as commodities based on their utility ('good for nothing'), confirming their status as property rather than legal persons.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire enslaved population, a large but specific segment of society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to public displays of deference
proof: [§22] Is any one exalted to the office of tribune? All who meet him congratulate him. One kisses his eyes, another his neck, and the slaves his hands. He goes to his house; finds it illuminated. He ascends the capitol; offers a sacrifice.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The passage details the specific ritualized honors and public deference (illumination, sacrifices, physical gestures of respect) legally or customarily granted to those holding the office of tribune.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies only to a very small number of high-ranking political officials.

proof: [§22] A person was talking with me to-day about applying for the priesthood in the temple of Augustus. I said to him, Let the thing alone, friend; you will be at great expense for nothing.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Seeking a high-level priesthood (in the temple of Augustus) required significant personal expenditure, functioning as a wealth-based barrier to access the role.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the wealthy elite seeking specific religious offices.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to have name recorded in official annals
proof: [§22] But my name, said he, will be written in the annals.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Holding a priesthood granted the specific right to have one's name recorded in the state's historical records (annals), a concrete civic privilege of the office.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Only the few individuals elected or appointed to priesthoods receive this benefit.

proof: [§25] This is the dispute between Jews and Syrians and Egyptians and Romans,—not whether the right be preferable to all things, and in every instance to be sought; but whether the eating swine’s flesh be consistent with right, or not.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus identifies the prohibition of eating pork as a defining legal/customary dispute specific to the Jews in contrast to other contemporary groups like Romans or Syrians.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The rule applies to an entire ethnic/religious group within the Roman Empire.

proof: [§26] Then why do you, Epicurus, dissuade a wise man from bringing up children? Why are you afraid that upon their account he may fall into anxieties? ... And does he, who knows all this, dare to forbid us to bring up children?
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus argues against the Epicurean school's customary rule that 'wise men' (philosophers) should avoid the burdens of raising children, asserting instead a natural obligation to do so.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific sub-culture of intellectuals/philosophers within the society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to engage in public business
proof: [§26] On the same grounds he says that a wise man will not engage himself in public business, knowing very well what must follow.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text references the Epicurean rule for 'wise men' to avoid civic and political life to maintain tranquility.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Relates to the specific social group of Epicurean practitioners.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to sit in senatorial amphitheater seats
proof: [§28] But I want to sit where the senators do. Do not you see, that by this you incommode and torment yourself? Why, how else shall I see the show in the Amphitheatre advantageously? ... Or wait a little while; and when the show is over, go sit in the senators’ places and sun yourself.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a concrete spatial restriction in the Roman amphitheater where specific high-status seating is reserved for senators and barred to others during the performance.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This defines a clear boundary between the senatorial class and the rest of the viewing public.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to retain consular robes and office
proof: [§27] If some tyrant bids you put off your consular robe,—Well, I am in my equestrian robe. Put off that too. I have only my coat.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text acknowledges the power of a tyrant to strip an individual of their high-ranking political office (Consul) and the physical symbols associated with it.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Consuls are a very small, specific elite group in Roman society.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement to specific cities
proof: [§28] You are forbidden to live at Nicopolis. Then I will not live there. Nor at Athens. Well, nor at Athens. Nor at Rome. Nor at Rome. But you shall live at Gyaros.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text illustrates the legal reality of banishment (relegatio/exile), where individuals are barred from centers of power and forced to reside in remote locations like Gyaros.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to individuals sentenced to various degrees of exile.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to lead roles in tragedies
proof: [§27] No poor man fills any other place in one [a tragedy] than as part of the chorus; whereas, kings begin indeed with prosperity.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus states that in the cultural/customary medium of drama, poor characters are restricted to the chorus, while lead roles are reserved for the elite (kings/tyrants).
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to a large portion of the population defined by low wealth.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to study philosophy without parental consent
proof: [§29] It is ridiculous, then, to say we must begin with these applications, for it is not easy to begin with the most difficult; and this excuse children should make to those parents who dislike that they should study philosophy.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text implies that parents have the authority to prevent or 'dislike' their children studying philosophy, requiring children to provide formal excuses or justifications to overcome this domestic hierarchy.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a broad category of the population (youth/children) within the domestic sphere of Greek/Roman society.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to seek admiration from senators
proof: [§29] But he who reads these things, and applies to the philosophers, merely for the sake of showing, at some entertainment, that he understands hypothetical reasonings, what aim has he but to be admired by some senator, who happens to sit near him?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a social practice where individuals use philosophical displays at entertainments to gain the attention and favor of senators, indicating a path for the wealthy or educated to access high-level political social circles.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This describes a specific subset of the population looking to climb the social ladder through intellectual display.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to legal advocacy in estate disputes
proof: [§30] Even if the business were concerning an estate, I should call in another advocate. To what advocate, then, shall I now appeal? I will leave it to any one who may be upon the spot.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus references the standard legal practice where individuals involved in property or estate disputes hire professional advocates to represent them, confirming the availability of legal counsel for those with resources.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Legal advocacy is a specific professional service used by those with specific property interests.

extracted_rules.resource: Protection from being led away as captives in war
proof: [§31] But when wives and children are led away captives, and the men themselves killed, are not these evils?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author lists the capture of wives and children and the killing of men during war as standard 'evils' (events) of his time, reflecting the lack of legal or customary protection for these groups during military defeat.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: War and its consequences (captvity/death) are general risks for entire populations in this era.

extracted_rules.resource: Access to objective measurement rules for goods
proof: [§31] When we would judge of light and heavy, we do not judge by guess, nor when we judge of straight and crooked; and, in general, when it concerns us to know the truth on any special point, no one of us will do anything by guess.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text establishes that in matters of material judgment (weight, straightness), society uses objective balances and rules rather than guesswork, protecting the integrity of transactions and property assessment.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to almost everyone participating in the economy.

proof: [§32] Accordingly, when a tyrant threatens, and sends for me, I say, Against what is your threatening pointed? [...] If he says, I will take away your estate. [...] Take my body; take my possessions; take my reputation; take away even my friends.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text acknowledges the real-world power of tyrants and 'the great' to seize the estates and possessions of individuals under their command, treating this as a standard (though according to the author, 'indifferent') occurrence in the Roman imperial context.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies generally to any subject of a tyrant or high official, which encompasses most people in that political system.

proof: [§32] If he says, will chain you, I answer, It is my hands and feet that you threaten. If he says, will cut off your head, I answer, It is my head that you threaten. If he says, will throw you into prison, I answer, It is the whole of this paltry body that you threaten.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus describes the capacity of political authorities (tyrants/kings/the great) to arbitrarily chain, imprison, or execute individuals as an established material reality of his society.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule describes the vulnerability of the general population to the whims of the ruling power.

proof: [§32] Even among the gladiators of Caesar, there are some who bear it very ill that they are not brought upon the stage and matched; and who offer vows to God, and address the officers, begging to fight.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The mention of gladiators needing to 'beg the officers' to fight shows that the timing and occurrence of their life-threatening labor are controlled by military or imperial officials, not the individuals themselves.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the specific occupational group of gladiators within the imperial household/service.

proof: [§32] But some one in authority has given a sentence. I judge you to be impious and profane. What has befallen you?—I have been judged to be impious and profane.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any subject could be judged, the context specifically addresses the targeting of those with distinct 'principles' or philosophers.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of movement to public entertainment
proof: [§32] But it is shameful to take only such view of things as truant slaves take of a play. We ought to sit calmly, and listen... and not do like those poor fellows, who come in and admire the actor, constantly glancing about them, and then, if any one happens to mention their master, run frightened away.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The description of 'truant slaves' (slaves away from their master's post without permission) shows they are legally and socially barred from attending public theaters/plays, as evidenced by their fear of being discovered and fleeing at the mention of their master.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This specific social behavior and restriction apply specifically to the enslaved population who are not under direct supervision.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to remain present during terminal rituals
proof: [§32] Was it to him that Socrates said, For this reason we sent the women out of the way? No, but to his friends,—to such as were capable of hearing it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus references the removal of women from the scene of Socrates' death as a normative practice in his time (and the historical past he cites), implying women were excluded from certain solemn or 'rational' final discourses/scenes.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to half the population (women) in specific ritualistic or philosophical contexts.

proof: [§34] For in this case we are not to give credit to the many, who say that none ought to be educated but the free; but rather to the philosophers, who say that the wise alone are free. [...] How shall we believe you, then, good legislators, when you say, We allow none to be educated but the free?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text explicitly mentions a prevailing social and legal norm (attributed to 'the many' and 'legislators') that limits education exclusively to those of free status.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to a broad demographic division of the population (free vs. enslaved) in Roman society.

proof: [§34] How shall we believe you, then, good legislators, when you say, We allow none to be educated but the free?
extracted_rules.reasoning: By stating that only the free are allowed to be educated, the text confirms that slaves are systematically excluded from formal education by the 'legislators'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire enslaved population, a significant group in this society.

proof: [§34] What, then; when any person hath turned his slave about before the consul, has he done nothing? Yes, he has. What? He has turned his slave about before the consul. Nothing more? Yes. He pays a fine for him. Well, then; is not the man who has gone through this ceremony rendered free?
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes the specific Roman legal ceremony of 'manumissio vindicta' involving a consul, which changes a slave's legal status to free.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While any slave can theoretically be freed, it requires a specific legal action by a master before a high official.

proof: [§34] Again, is not the theatre common to all the citizens? Therefore come, when all are seated, if you dare, and turn any one of them out of his place.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author uses the theater as an example of a resource held in common by citizens, where an individual citizen's right to their specific place is protected against others.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to the entire body of citizens attending public events.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to hold social offices or trust
proof: [§34] But if, being a man, you cannot fill any one place in human society, what shall we do with you? For, suppose you cannot hold the place of a friend, can you hold even that of a slave? [...] Why, then, should not you also be contented to be thrown upon some dunghill, as a useless vessel
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a man found guilty of adultery as having lost his 'rank' and being unfit for any role in society, including that of a citizen, neighbor, or even a trusted slave.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific sub-group of individuals who have been legally or socially convicted of a specific breach of fidelity.

proof: [§34] Pray, sir, shall I inherit my father’s estate? Let us see; let us sacrifice upon the occasion.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all legitimate sons within the social structure.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to the entire class of enslaved persons within the society.

proof: [§35] For in this case we are not to give credit to the many, who say that none ought to be educated but the free; [...] How shall we believe you, then, good legislators, when you say, We allow none to be educated but the free?

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to all individuals possessing the status of 'free'.

proof: [§36] If it be necessary to embrace his knees, do so; if to weep, weep; if to groan, groan For when you have once made yourself a slave to externals, be a slave wholly; do not struggle, and be alternately willing and unwilling

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual regardless of status when they become a defendant in a court of law.

extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus describes adultery not just as a moral failing but as a violation of 'the laws' which distribute domestic relations. extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies to all married individuals and neighbors within the civic community.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Banishment was a specific legal penalty applied to individuals of certain status or those involved in political/legal disputes.

proof: [§40] My life is in danger from Caesar. ... The instrument is either a sword, or a wheel, or the sea, or a tile, or a tyrant; and what does it signify to you by what way you descend to Hades? All are equal; but, if you would hear the truth, the shortest is that by which a tyrant sends you.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus discusses the reality of living under an authority (Caesar/Tyrant) who has the legal or de facto power to put subjects to death via various methods (sword, wheel, etc.).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to personal autonomy in combat

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies specifically to those currently serving in a military capacity under a commander.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to receive private material support
proof: [§41] Therefore the lady who was going to send a month’s provision to Gratilla, in her banishment, made a right answer to one who told her that Domitian would seize it. I had rather, said she, that he should seize it, than I not send it.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary or semi-legal practice where friends of an exiled person (Gratilla) send provisions to sustain them. While the emperor (Domitian) might seize it, the act of sending it is treated as a social duty/right that exists despite the banishment.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the specific sub-group of individuals under political banishment (exiles) and their access to external resources.

proof: [§41] What, then, is it that leads us so often to divination? Cowardice; the dread of events. Hence we flatter the diviners. Pray, sir, shall I inherit my father’s estate? Let us see; let us sacrifice upon the occasion.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text identifies inheriting a father's estate as a standard expectation and legal concern for men in this society, to the point of seeking diviners' help to confirm it.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Inheritance of paternal property is a general rule applying to a large portion of the male citizen population.

proof: [§42] Why was an ass made? Was it as being of primary importance? No; but because we had need of a back able to carry burdens. ... for, if an understanding of that use had been likewise added, he would not, in reason, have been subject to us, nor have done us these services, but would have been like and equal to ourselves.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The author contrasts humans with animals (specifically asses) based on the legal and customary fact of the animal's subjection and utility for labor, defined by their lack of 'understanding'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies broadly to all work animals used in the economy.

extracted_rules.resource: Recognition as a full member of the group

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any non-Jew seeking membership in that specific ethnic/religious community.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to choice of dress and grooming

extracted_rules.reasoning: The text indicates that students of philosophy are expected to adhere to specific standards of appearance and conduct (avoiding slovenliness and finery) as part of their training and public role.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific subset of the population (young men in philosophical schools).

extracted_rules.resource: Right to private property and independent speech
proof: [§44] Remember next, that perhaps you are a son, and what does this character imply? extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus defines the social role of a son as one of total subordination to the father, involving the lack of private property ('everything... belonging to his father') and the loss of independent agency or critical speech.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a very large segment of the male population under the Roman concept of patria potestas.

proof: [§44] After this, know likewise, that you are a brother too; and that to this character it belongs to make concessions, to be easily persuaded, to use gentle language, never to claim for yourself any non-essential thing, but cheerfully to give up these to be repaid by a larger share of things essential.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a customary rule where a brother is expected to yield material goods (non-essential things) to maintain familial harmony, effectively reducing his individual claim to certain property.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Applies to any male with siblings, a common but specific familial role.

extracted_rules.resource: Freedom of behavior personal to the individual

proof: [§46] But what then? This is no very safe affair now, and especially at Rome. For he who does it must not do it in a corner, but go to some rich consular senator, for instance, and question him. I myself was once a great admirer of this method of instruction, till I fell into this kind of adventures.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus notes that philosophers or teachers using the Socratic method on high-ranking officials lack protection from physical violence ('a box on the ear') if they offend their social superiors.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those engaged in public philosophical disputation with elites.

proof: [§46] This is no very safe affair now, and especially at Rome. For he who does it must not do it in a corner, but go to some rich consular senator, for instance, and question him. [...] Here, now, comes the danger that he may first say, Pray, good sir, what business is that of yours? What are you to me? Then if you persist in troubling him, he may lift up his hand and give you a box on the ear.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text describes a social reality in Rome where high-ranking individuals are effectively immune to being questioned by social inferiors, backed by the threat of unpunished physical retaliation.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Consular senators represent the absolute peak of the Roman social hierarchy, a very small group.

proof: [§47] When, therefore, you go into the field on horseback, are you anxious on being matched against one who is on foot,—you being practised and he unpractised? Ay, but the person has power to kill me. Then speak the truth, O unfortunate! and be not arrogant, nor take the philosopher upon you, nor conceal from yourself who are your masters; but while you are thus to be held by the body, follow the strongest.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus uses the master-slave dynamic to explain physical anxiety. He acknowledges that 'masters' have the literal 'power to kill' those held 'by the body,' reflecting the legal reality of the Roman era where slaves lacked protection from capital punishment by their owners.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Slaves represented a significant portion of the Roman population, making this a widely applicable status-based rule.

proof: [§47] Socrates, indeed, had studied how to speak, who talked in such a manner to tyrants and judges, and in prison. Diogenes had studied how to speak, who talked in such a manner to Alexander, to Philip, to the pirates, to the person who bought him. This belonged to those who had studied the matter; who had courage. But do you go where you belong and remain there. Retire into some corner, and there sit and weave syllogisms, and propose them to others. For there is not in you a man who can rule the city.
extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus states that the ability to 'rule the city' is reserved for those who have mastered a specific kind of 'courage' and rhetorical study. He explicitly tells those who only know theoretical logic (weaving syllogisms) to stay in their 'corner,' denying them the resource of political leadership.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a specific class of educated but non-statesman individuals, a mid-sized segment of the elite/sub-elite.

proof: [§48] Well; but it is a grievous thing for a man to be confuted who has grown old, and has perhaps served through his three campaigns to a senatorship. I know it very well. You are rich; and perhaps have a wife and children, and a great number of domestics.
extracted_rules.reasoning: The text links the achievement of a 'senatorship' and serving 'three campaigns' with being 'rich' and having 'a great number of domestics.' It reflects the Roman census requirements where only those of a certain wealth and lineage/service record could access the Senate.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: The Senatorial class was an extremely small, specific elite group within the Roman Empire.

extracted_rules.resource: Right to understand and use domestic laws

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Foreigners and resident aliens were a common but distinct group in Roman cities.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to all individuals recognized as citizens within the 'lesser city' (the state) and the 'greater city' (the cosmos).

proof: [§50] Is it Thy will that I should be in a public or a private condition; dwell here, or be banished; be poor, or rich?

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While citizenship is broad, the actual holding of public office was restricted to a smaller subset of the citizen body.

proof: [§50] Is it Thy will that I should be in a public or a private condition; dwell here, or be banished; be poor, or rich?

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Banishment was a common legal penalty applicable to the citizen class in the Roman Empire.

proof: [§50] Is it Thy will that I should be in a public or a private condition; dwell here, or be banished; be poor, or rich?

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Economic status affects everyone in the society.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: Orators are a specific professional class within the educated elite.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to a very specific professional group (musicians/performers).

proof: [§51] Give me but one young man who brings this intention with him to the school, who is a champion for this point, and says, yield up all the rest; it suffices me, if once I become able to pass my life free from hindrance and grief... extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While triggered by a certain state of mind, the rule describes a broad social institution (temple supplication) available to those in that state.

proof: [§52] To-day, when I saw a handsome person, I did not say to myself, Oh. that I could possess her! and how happy is her husband (for he who says this, says too, how happy is her gallant)

proof: [§53] Why did you assume plumage not your own? Why did you call yourself a Stoic? Observe yourselves thus in your actions, and you will find of what sect you are. You will find that most of you are Epicureans; a few are Peripatetics, and those but loose ones.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This rule applies specifically to the community of students and practitioners of philosophy in Rome/Greece, a significant but distinct subgroup of the educated population.

proof: [§53] I am now your preceptor, and you come to be instructed by me. And indeed my aim is to secure you from being restrained, compelled, hindered; to make you free, prosperous, happy; looking to God upon every occasion, great or small.

proof: [§55] You have been fighting at home with your manservant; you have turned the house upside-down, and alarmed the neighborhood.

proof: [§56] Throw a bit of an estate betwixt you and your son, and you will see that he will quickly wish you under ground, and you him; and then you, no doubt, on the other hand will exclaim, What a son have I brought up! He would bury me alive!

proof: [§56] The wise person alone, then, is capable of loving. ... If, therefore, I rest my personality in the will, then only shall I be a friend, a son, or a father, such as I ought. ... extracted_rules.reasoning: Epictetus defines the role of 'friend', 'son', and 'father' as statuses dependent on a specific philosophical disposition (the will). Those who lack this (the unwise/bad men) are categorized as incapable of these social relations, treating them instead as 'utensils'.
extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: While a philosophical distinction, it categorizes large swaths of the population as incapable of genuine legal/customary fidelity.

proof: [§56] It is no principle of humanity that makes them bite and vilify each other ... and convert courts of justice into dens of robbers; that prompts them to be intemperate, adulterers, seducers; or leads them into other offences that men commit against each other.

proof: [§56] It is no principle of humanity that makes them bite and vilify each other, and take possession of public assemblies, as wild beasts do of solitudes and mountains.

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This refers to the general conduct of political life in Greek and Roman cities described by the author.

proof: [§57] There is some use in an ass, though not so much as in an ox; and in a dog, though not so much as in a servant; and in a servant, though not so much as in the citizens; and in the citizens, though not so much as in the magistrates.

proof: [§57] There is some use in an ass, though not so much as in an ox; and in a dog, though not so much as in a servant; and in a servant, though not so much as in the citizens; and in the citizens, though not so much as in the magistrates.

proof: [§57] There is some use in an ass, though not so much as in an ox; and in a dog, though not so much as in a servant; and in a servant, though not so much as in the citizens; and in the citizens, though not so much as in the magistrates.

proof: [§58] This seems to be universally confessed, that he who would hear philosophers needs some kind of exercise in hearing. Is it not so? Tell me, then, on what I shall speak to you. How shall I show you that contradiction among the generality of mankind, by which they differ concerning good and evil, profitable and unprofitable, when you know not what contradiction means?

extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies specifically to those seeking higher education or philosophical mentorship, which was a distinct subgroup in Roman-era society.

proof: [§58] Thus we too have certain natural inclinations to speaking, when the hearer appears to be somebody, when he gives us encouragement; but if he sits by like a stone or a tuft of grass, how can he excite any desire in a man? ... extracted_rules.specificity_reasoning: This applies to any individual attempting to engage in formal debate or reasoned defense.
